vignette: A 55-year-old male with cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B, MELD 18) presents with hematemesis. Vitals: BP 85/50, HR 120. He is confused. Exam shows ascites and spider angiomata. What is the FIRST step in management?

options.text: IV octreotide + IV ceftriaxone + IV fluids

explanation.whyCorrect: Variceal hemorrhage: 1) IV fluids, 2) IV octreotide, 3) IV ceftriaxone. Then urgent endoscopy within 12 hours.

whyWrong.E: PPIs are for PEPTIC ulcer bleeding.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Variceal bleed: Octreotide + Ceftriaxone + Fluids → Urgent EGD (banding).

vignette: A 42-year-old female presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting after a fatty meal. Lipase is 1,200 U/L. CT shows acute interstitial pancreatitis with no necrosis. What is the MOST important next step?

options.text: Start early enteral nutrition within 24 hours

explanation.whyCorrect: Early enteral nutrition (within 24-48 hours) reduces mortality, infections, and length of stay.

whyWrong.C: NPO and TPN are outdated. Enteral nutrition is superior.
whyWrong.D: ERCP is for gallstone pancreatitis with cholangitis.
whyWrong.E: Surgery is for infected necrosis after 4 weeks.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Pancreatitis: Aggressive IV fluids + Early enteral nutrition = Reduced mortality.

vignette: A 28-year-old female presents with jaundice, fatigue, and arthralgias. Labs: AST 180, ALT 220, ALP 150, Total bilirubin 4.5. ANA positive (1:320), Anti-smooth muscle antibody positive. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Young female + Hepatitis pattern + ANA + ASMA positive = Autoimmune hepatitis Type 1.

whyWrong.A: PBC has cholestatic pattern + AMA positive.
whyWrong.C: PSC has cholestatic pattern + MRCP shows beaded ducts.
whyWrong.D: Drug-induced would not have positive autoimmune markers.
whyWrong.E: Wilson presents with neuropsychiatric symptoms + KF rings.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Autoimmune hepatitis: Young female + Hepatitis pattern + ANA/ASMA positive. Treatment: Steroids + Azathioprine.

vignette: A 50-year-old male at average risk for colorectal cancer asks about screening options. He prefers a non-invasive test. Which is the MOST appropriate recommendation?

explanation.whyCorrect: CRC screening starts at 45. Non-invasive option: FIT annually. If positive → colonoscopy.

whyWrong.A: Colonoscopy is invasive. Patient requested non-invasive.
whyWrong.C: CT colonography is an option but FIT is preferred.
whyWrong.D: Flex sig is less common now.

explanation.clinicalPearl: CRC screening: Start at 45! Options: Colonoscopy q10yr, FIT q1yr.

vignette: A 48-year-old male with history of heartburn for 10 years presents with progressive dysphagia to solids. EGD shows distal esophageal stricture and biopsy shows intestinal metaplasia without dysplasia. What is the BEST long-term management?

options.text: Endoscopic surveillance every 3-5 years + PPI BID
Endoscopic surveillance every 1 year + PPI daily

explanation.whyCorrect: Barrett esophagus WITHOUT dysplasia: Surveillance every 3-5 years + PPI BID.

whyWrong.B: EMR is for visible lesions or high-grade dysplasia.
whyWrong.D: Annual surveillance is for low-grade dysplasia.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Non-dysplastic Barrett: PPI BID + surveillance q3-5yr.

vignette: A 45-year-old obese female presents with episodic RUQ pain after fatty meals for 3 months. US shows multiple gallstones, CBD 4mm, no wall thickening. LFTs are normal. What is the BEST management?

explanation.whyCorrect: Symptomatic cholelithiasis: Elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy is definitive treatment.

whyWrong.B: ERCP is for choledocholithiasis or cholangitis.
whyWrong.D: Symptomatic gallstones should be treated surgically.
whyWrong.E: Emergency surgery is for acute cholecystitis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Symptomatic cholelithiasis: Elective cholecystectomy. Acute cholecystitis: Early cholecystectomy (within 72h).

vignette: A 28-year-old male with ulcerative colitis (pancolitis) for 5 years presents with worsening bloody diarrhea, urgency, and weight loss. He has failed mesalamine and azathioprine. Colonoscopy shows severe active colitis. What is the BEST next step?

explanation.whyCorrect: Severe UC flare failing mesalamine + thiopurine: Start steroids (prednisone or IV methylprednisolone) for rapid symptom control + Anti-TNF (infliximab) for long-term remission. Anti-TNFs work faster than vedolizumab.

whyWrong.A: Anti-TNF alone may not be sufficient for severe acute flare.
whyWrong.B: Already failed azathioprine, increasing dose unlikely to help.
whyWrong.C: Vedolizumab has slower onset (6-12 weeks), not ideal for severe flare.
whyWrong.D: Surgery is last resort after failing medical therapy (3 biologics).

explanation.clinicalPearl: Severe UC flare: Steroids (bridge) + Anti-TNF (infliximab). Vedolizumab for maintenance. Surgery if refractory.

vignette: A 45-year-old male presents with jaundice, fatigue, and RUQ pain for 1 week. He ate at a seafood restaurant 4 weeks ago. Labs: AST 1200, ALT 1500, ALP 200, Bilirubin 8.0. Hepatitis serologies: HAV IgM positive. What is the expected outcome?

options.text: Progression to chronic hepatitis in 80% of cases
Acute hepatitis with complete recovery in >99% of cases

explanation.whyCorrect: Acute Hepatitis A: Fecal-oral transmission (contaminated seafood). Self-limited, complete recovery in >99%. NO chronic carrier state. Treatment: Supportive. Prevention: HAV vaccine.

whyWrong.A: HAV does NOT cause chronic hepatitis (unlike HBV and HCV).
whyWrong.C: Fulminant hepatitis is rare (<1%), mostly in elderly or underlying liver disease.
whyWrong.D: HCC risk is with chronic HBV/HCV, not acute HAV.
whyWrong.E: HAV does not progress to cirrhosis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Hepatitis A: Acute only, NEVER chronic. Fecal-oral spread. Complete recovery in >99%. Vaccine available.

vignette: A 30-year-old female presents with chronic diarrhea, weight loss, and megaloblastic anemia. She has a history of recurrent oral ulcers. Small bowel biopsy shows villous atrophy with increased intraepithelial lymphocytes. Which HLA type is MOST strongly associated?

explanation.whyCorrect: Celiac disease: Villous atrophy + intraepithelial lymphocytosis. HLA-DQ2 (90-95%) or HLA-DQ8 (5-10%) is present in >99% of patients. High negative predictive value (if negative, rules out celiac). Associated with dermatitis herpetiformis.

whyWrong.A: HLA-B27: Ankylosing spondylitis, reactive arthritis, IBD-associated arthritis.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Celiac disease: HLA-DQ2/DQ8. Villous atrophy + IEL. Treatment: Strict gluten-free diet. Check tTG-IgA for screening.

vignette: A 70-year-old male presents with painless jaundice, weight loss, and a palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier sign). CT shows a 3cm mass in the head of the pancreas with biliary dilation. CA 19-9 is elevated. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

explanation.whyCorrect: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma: Painless jaundice + Courvoisier sign (palpable, non-tender gallbladder) + Weight loss + Mass in head of pancreas. Most common pancreatic malignancy. CA 19-9 is tumor marker (not diagnostic). Treatment: Whipple procedure if resectable.

whyWrong.A: Pseudocyst has history of pancreatitis, not painless jaundice.
whyWrong.B: Chronic pancreatitis causes pain, calcifications, not mass with obstruction.
whyWrong.D: PNETs are less common, often functional (insulin, gastrin), and may not cause jaundice.
whyWrong.E: AIP responds to steroids, has IgG4 elevation, and diffuse 'sausage-shaped' pancreas.

explanation.clinicalPearl: Painless jaundice + Courvoisier sign + Pancreatic head mass = Adenocarcinoma until proven otherwise. Only 15-20% resectable at diagnosis.
